ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general curry rice node (FOODON_00002809); no specific beef yakiniku curry rice node exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKK covers curry rice as a composite dish; no dedicated beef yakiniku curry rice code exists. Closest defensible parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for this specific composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of beef yakiniku curry rice is approximately 350 g, comprising a mound of steamed white rice topped with Japanese curry sauce and grilled marinated beef slices.

nutrient_source.title: Beef Yakiniku Curry Rice — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Beef Yakiniku Curry Rice (record G00010219). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken from this canonical HPB record. Saturated fat is not reported by HPB for this entry and has been estimated from USDA FoodData Central beef and curry rice component data; see notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; University of Sydney GI Database — white rice and Japanese curry rice analogous entries
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Beef Yakiniku Curry Rice. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on the dominant carbohydrate component being white jasmine/short-grain rice (GI ~64–72, University of Sydney GI Database) combined with the moderating effect of the curry sauce, dietary fibre (3.7 g/100g), protein (6.5 g/100g), and fat (4.21 g/100g). A composite GI of 65 is a defensible central estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (65 × 88.2 g carbohydrate in 350 g serving) / 100 = 57.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100g from soy sauce and curry roux; partially offset by potassium 327 mg/100g
hypertension.guidance: A 350 g serving delivers approximately 809 mg sodium (35% of the 2300 mg daily limit); individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium soy sauce in the yakiniku marinade, limit portion size, and avoid adding extra condiments to keep total daily sodium within target.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white rice base (GI ~65) with 25.2 g carbohydrate/100g; partially moderated by 3.7 g dietary fibre/100g
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should substitute white rice with brown rice or cauliflower rice to lower the glycaemic response, request a reduced-sugar yakiniku marinade to minimise added sugars from mirin, and limit the serving to 200–250 g to keep the glycaemic load within a manageable range.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from beef (~1.6 g/100g estimated) and sodium 231 mg/100g; iron 2.55 mg/100g is beneficial
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to manage saturated fat and sodium intake, choose lean cuts of beef or substitute with chicken or fish to reduce saturated fat load, and use reduced-sodium soy sauce in the marinade to support blood pressure control.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beef is a red meat high in arachidonic acid, promoting pro-inflammatory cytokine production
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should substitute beef with oily fish (salmon, mackerel) to shift the omega-6/omega-3 balance toward anti-inflammatory pathways, and monitor for symptom flares associated with nightshade vegetables (potato) present in the curry.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef Yakiniku Curry Rice is a Japanese-style composite dish served in Singapore, consisting of steamed white rice topped with Japanese-style curry sauce and slices of grilled marinated beef (yakiniku). The beef is marinated in a soy sauce, mirin, garlic, and ginger mixture before being grilled, imparting a savoury-sweet caramelised flavour. The curry sauce is a mild-to-medium Japanese curry made from a roux base with onion, potato, and carrot, producing a thick, lightly spiced gravy that is distinctly sweeter and milder than South Asian curries. The dish delivers 164 kcal per 100 g, with 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat, and 25.19 g carbohydrate, supported by 3.7 g dietary fibre and 2.55 mg iron per 100 g. Sodium is moderate at 231 mg/100g, partially offset by a favourable potassium level of 327 mg/100g. The glycaemic index is estimated at 65 (medium) due to the white rice base, moderated by fibre, protein, and fat from the curry and beef components. Clinically, this dish is of moderate concern for hypertension (sodium load from soy sauce and curry roux), type 2 diabetes (high-GI white rice base), and cardiovascular disease (saturated fat from beef). It is not suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to the pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid content of red meat. The dish is a popular lunch and dinner option in Japanese casual dining restaurants in Singapore.
common_user_queries: Is beef yakiniku curry rice healthy?
How many calories in beef yakiniku curry rice?

Can I eat beef yakiniku curry rice if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry (G00010219, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central beef and curry rice component data and is clearly documented. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using the white rice dominant-carbohydrate method, which is the standard defensible approach for composite rice dishes without a direct GI measurement.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.6 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central beef stir-fry and Japanese curry rice component data (USDA SR Legacy entries for cooked beef and curry sauce); HPB does not report saturated fat for this entry. This value should be replaced with a direct measurement when available. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, fat-soluble vitamins, B-vitamin panel, and moisture data for this composite dish are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for G00010219, and no sufficiently close composite match exists in USDA FoodData Central to provide defensible values for all these fields. 3. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 by HPB; this is plausible for a composite dish where the beef contribution is diluted across the full dish weight and the HPB analytical method may report below-detection-threshold values as zero. Confirmed zero per HPB source. 4. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on the white rice dominant-carbohydrate method using University of Sydney GI Database reference values for white rice (GI 64–72); the moderating effect of 3.7 g/100g fibre, 6.5 g/100g protein, and 4.21 g/100g fat is factored into the central estimate. 5. Halal status is not confirmed; the dish contains beef and soy sauce and is served in a Japanese casual dining context in Singapore — halal certification should be verified with the specific restaurant operator.
